title: Beer-Battered Haddock
subtitle: Fresh haddock in crisp, cold beer batter, fried in hot oil until deeply golden
excerpt: Beer-battered haddock works because the carbon dioxide in the beer creates lightness in the batter, and the cold temperature keeps it from building gluten, producing a shell that fries crisp rather than bready. The method is straightforward but the oil temperature is the one thing that cannot be neglected.
sourceNotes: British chippy tradition. Beer-battered fish has been documented in British fish-and-chip cookery from the late 19th century. The beer batter variant became the pub-standard version in the 20th century, offering a lighter, crisper result than plain flour batter.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the batter up to 30 minutes ahead and keep chilled.
recipe.temperatureNote: Fry in oil at 180°C on the hob; no oven temperature required.

glossaryTerms.definition: To cook food fully submerged in oil at 170 to 190°C. The hot oil seals the coating instantly, trapping moisture inside and forming a crisp crust. Maintaining the correct temperature is essential: if the oil is too cool, food absorbs oil and becomes greasy rather than crisp.

content.text: Beer-battered haddock is a pub and chippy standard. The beer serves two purposes: its carbon dioxide bubbles through the batter as it fries, creating a light, open texture, and the alcohol inhibits gluten formation, keeping the crust crisp rather than chewy. Use cold beer straight from the fridge. Keep the oil at temperature. The fish takes under 5 minutes.

items.prepNote: 150 g for the batter, the rest for dusting

content.text: Make the batter and heat the oil

Whisk 150 g of the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour, the baking powder, and a generous pinch of salt in a bowl. Pour in the {{beer}} of cold beer and whisk to a smooth batter about the consistency of double cream. Keep it chilled until needed.

Pour the {{vegetable-oil}} of vegetable oil into a large, heavy saucepan to a depth of at least 8 cm. Heat over a medium-high heat until it reaches 180°C on a thermometer. Keep the heat steady throughout frying.

Dry the {{haddock-fillet}} haddock fillets thoroughly with kitchen paper and season well. Dust each fillet lightly in the remaining flour, shaking off the excess. Dip into the batter, let the excess drip off for a moment, then lower carefully into the hot oil.
Cook for 4 to 5 minutes, turning once, until the batter is deeply golden and the fish floats freely. Do not crowd the pan;
in two batches if needed, letting the oil return to 180°C between batches. Drain on a wire rack and season immediately with salt.

items.cause: Oil was below 180°C when the fish went in.
items.fix: Check the temperature before each batch. The oil drops when cold fish enters the pan; let it return to 180°C before adding the next fillet.

items.cause: Fillets weren't dried before dusting, or the batter was too thin.
items.fix: Pat the fish completely dry with kitchen paper before flouring. The flour gives the batter something to grip. A well-chilled, slightly thick batter also holds better than a warm, thin one.

items.symptom: Batter is thick and bready rather than crisp
items.cause: The beer was warm, activating the gluten.
items.fix: Use beer straight from the fridge. If the batter looks thick and doughy, thin it with a tablespoon of cold sparkling water.

items.symptom: Fish is raw in the centre
items.cause: Fillets were too thick or the oil temperature was too high, browning the outside before the inside cooked.
items.fix: For thick fillets, hold the oil at 170°C and fry for 6 to 7 minutes. Flake the thickest part with a fork to check.

content.text: Fish and chips was documented as a distinct British dish by the 1860s. The fish component derives from a tradition of Jewish fried fish brought to Britain by Sephardic immigrants, who fried fish in a thin flour batter. The beer batter version became the pub-standard variant in the 20th century. The haddock is the northern English preference; cod dominates in the south. Both work identically in this recipe.
